Numpy's vectorised datetime functionality (https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/arrays.datetime.html) does a better job of handling all of the various date and time corrections and gives quite a large speedup. It wasn't available in Python at the time I wrote these modules. The downside of datetime is that it requires that the year is greater than or equal to 1. It is not totally uncommon for a user to want to run the hmtk on catalogues that contain historical events and instrumental ones, which may contain events Before Common Era (represented by negative numbers). Whilst I don't expect these to flag up aftershocks (not real ones anyway) if you wanted to port the code to datetime then one would need to account for this case.

I worked on the premise that setting a boolean flag is atomic. That is probably a mix of laziness and premature optimization. However to get the "right value" a mutex on disable sync will not be sufficient or in any way helpful. Instead touch_member needs to be protected by a mutex to avoid getting the value overwritten by stale one.

However I don't think it's worth the effort in this case - it would be better to add a timeout for while_not_sync_standby (2*loop_wait should be enough). Even if there is a race that makes master not notice in time, at worst there will be a small hickup on commits in the master. A timeout is much more robust solution than a locking scheme.

There is a second cluster wide race there anyway:

* Master picks standby to be sync
* Standby publishes nosync
* Standby checks sync state and thinks it can shut down.
* Master makes standby sync standby and publishes sync state
* Standby shuts down
* Master takes up to loop_wait to notice this.

It would be possible to guarantee no unnecessary sync standby waits with a considerable cost in complexity by adding a sync standby candidate field to sync state, have the master publish that and then run an API call to double check before calling synchronous_standby. Thoughts?

* It complicates code quite a lot.
* As a user I'd expect that `write_ext.redis` allows me to set similar parameters to the ones I can set through `write_ext.by_url`. That's true for all other datastores we implement here except for Kafka, for which we don't provide URL support at all.

On context closing (exit), workers responsible for checking problem status on SAPI and downloading of results are terminated -- and pending futures are *never* resolved.

I see your point, but I don't agree with it.  I don't think the root environment is necessarily on PATH.  I have been working to make that more achievable.  If paths to the activate scripts are provided absolutely, then there is no requirement, explicit or implicit, that root is active when starting a process.  Moreover, my thinking is that assuming no initial root env activation, we're in a better place.  When people want to work with conda and anaconda tools, they can start up a special "anaconda prompt" that drops them into the root environment (or directly into any other environment).  This is how Visual Studio does it.  People are still free to add the root environment to PATH.  If that's the case, then we never touch it - only prepend to PATH so earlier env gets found first.  This is less clean, because stuff not present in the prepended PATH may still get found in the later PATH.  I don't propose to fix this problem, but rather to provide tools towards the (cleaner) alternative.

Ah, the version I had was indeed a bit off and not Python 2.7 compatible. Here's an updated version:

```python
def _recombine_skipped_queue(tokens, skipped_idxs):
    """
    >>> tokens = ["foo", " ", "bar", " ", "19June2000", "baz"]
    >>> skipped_idxs = set([0, 1, 2, 5])
    >>> _recombine_skipped(tokens, skipped_idxs)
    ["foo bar", "baz"]

    """
    # This groups consecutive values
    skipped_tokens = []
    idx_queue = []
    for idx in skipped_idxs:
        if idx_queue and idx - 1 == [-1]:
        if len(idx_queue) and idx - 1 != idx_queue[-1]:
            skipped_tokens.append(''.join(map(tokens.__getitem__, idx_queue)))
            idx_queue = []

        idx_queue.append(idx)

    if idx_queue:
        skipped_tokens.append(''.join(map(tokens.__getitem__, idx_queue)))

    return skipped_tokens
```

Interestingly, I couldn't figure out why this was consistently slower than your version, since strings are immutable, I was expecting growing a string incrementally with `+` to be *much* slower than queuing up all the parts and combining them at the end, but lo and behold, if I switch over to using the `+=` style method, the list version is faster than your version! I believe that's because in CPython they've special-cased this sort of string extension to try to extend the string in-place. See [this StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4435169/good-way-to-append-to-a-string). Here's the faster version:

```python
def _recombine_skipped(tokens, skipped_idxs):
    """
    >>> tokens = ["foo", " ", "bar", " ", "19June2000", "baz"]
    >>> skipped_idxs = set([0, 1, 2, 5])
    >>> _recombine_skipped(tokens, skipped_idxs)
    ["foo bar", "baz"]

    """
    # This groups consecutive values
    skipped_tokens = []
    idx_queue = []
    for i, idx in enumerate(skipped_idxs):
        if i > 0 and idx - 1 == skipped_idxs[i - 1]:
            skipped_tokens[-1] = skipped_tokens[-1] + tokens[idx]
        else:
            skipped_tokens.append(tokens[idx])

    return skipped_tokens
```

This is ignoring a possible timezone attached to the datetime object. E.g. if the configuration value has been added with one in the settings, we need to make sure to respect that when instantiating the JavaScript Date object. That's especially important since we use `get_format` a bit below which can -- if `USE_L10N` is enabled -- render a timezone aware datetime in the current active timezone -- which depending on the project setup can and in my experience often is different from the server time.

Django solves this for the admin datetime widget by passing in the [UTC offset from the server side](https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/contrib/admin/templates/admin/base.html#L14-L15) and then uses it when it [constructs a new Date object](https://github.com/django/django/blob/3c447b108ac70757001171f7a4791f493880bf5b/django/contrib/admin/static/admin/js/admin/DateTimeShortcuts.js#L43-L53). I think we need to follow the same approach here to prevent accidentally setting the wrong value.
